1: A structural note: 'In the eleventh year in the third month on the first day of the month the word of YHWH came to me.' June 587 BCE — one month before Jerusalem falls (July 586 BCE). Ezekiel's lament for Egypt as the great cedar is delivered at the moment when both Jerusalem and Egypt are approaching their doom. The cedar oracle (Ezekiel 31:1-18) is one of Ezekiel's great sustained metaphors, comparable to the vine (Ezekiel 15), the eagle-and-vine (Ezekiel 17), and the dry bones (Ezekiel 37) in literary power and theological depth.
2: A structural note: 'Son of man, say to Pharaoh king of Egypt and to his multitude: Whom are you like in your greatness?' The rhetorical question — “whom are you like in your greatness?” — invites Pharaoh to compare himself to the greatest. The answer will be the cedar of Lebanon, the most majestic of trees. But the point is not admiration; it is warning: the greatest tree ever seen was cut down. No greatness is permanent under YHWH's sovereignty.
3: A type: 'Behold Assyria was a cedar in Lebanon with beautiful branches and forest shade, and of great height, its top among the clouds.' The cedar of Lebanon (erez hallevanon) is Ezekiel's image for imperial greatness. The cedar's “top among the clouds” echoes the Tower of Babel (Genesis 11:4: “a tower with its top in the heavens”) — the recurring image of human ambition that reaches for the sky. Against this, Christ is the one who actually came down from the heights (John 6:38: “I have come down from heaven”) — true greatness that descended, not false greatness that climbed.
4: A pattern: 'The waters nourished it; the deep made it grow tall, making its rivers flow around the place of its planting, sending forth its streams to all the trees of the field.' The abundant nourishment of Egypt's imperial wealth — YHWH controlling those very waters (Ezekiel 29:3). Every resource Egypt used to build its empire was ultimately YHWH's provision, diverted for imperial purposes. The parable of the talents (Matthew 25:14-30) makes the same point: all human capacity is entrusted, not owned.
5: A pattern: 'Therefore it towered high above all the trees of the field; its boughs grew large and its branches long from abundant water in its shoots.' The cedar grew taller than all others because of its unique water supply — its greatness was circumstantial, not essential. The same circumstantial quality applies to all human greatness: nations and empires are great because of geography, resources, timing — providentially supplied advantages. The humility required is to acknowledge this, as Daniel told Nebuchadnezzar: “the Most High God rules the kingdom of mankind and sets over it whom he will” (Daniel 5:21).
6: A pattern: 'All the birds of the heavens made their nests in its boughs; under its branches all the beasts of the field gave birth to their young, and under its shadow lived all great nations.' The great cedar as shelter for all creatures — the image of an imperial patron-tree. The image is reused in a positive Christological direction in the parable of the mustard seed (Matthew 13:32; Mark 4:32): “it becomes the greatest of all shrubs and becomes a tree so that the birds of the air come and make nests in its branches.” What Egypt's cedar was in shadow-form — a tree sheltering all nations — Christ's kingdom is in final and true form.
7: A pattern: 'It was beautiful in its greatness, in the length of its branches; for its roots went down to abundant water.' The beauty of the cedar is genuine — YHWH made the Nile, the cedars, the great natural world that Egypt drew upon. But beauty and greatness rooted in creation, unacknowledged as gift, become pride. The pattern warns that even genuine beauty becomes disordered when it is self-referential rather than doxological. Christ is the true beauty that does not become disordered: “full of grace and truth” (John 1:14).
8: A pattern: 'The cedars in the garden of God could not rival it, nor the fir trees equal its boughs; neither were the plane trees like its branches; no tree in the garden of God was its equal in beauty.' “The garden of God” — Eden — is the standard of comparison. The cosmic hyperbole positions Egypt as the greatest power in creation's entire history, setting up the judgment: if not even Eden's trees compare to this cedar, what can topple it? Only YHWH himself. The same logic applies to every claim to ultimate greatness: Christ alone exceeds all.
9: A pattern: 'I made it beautiful in the mass of its branches, and all the trees of Eden envied it that were in the garden of God.' “I made it beautiful” — YHWH takes credit for the cedar's beauty. Egypt's greatness was YHWH's creation, making its pride all the more inexcusable: the very beauty it turned into an idol was given by the one it displaced. Paul's “what do you have that you did not receive? If then you received it, why do you boast as if you did not receive it?” (1 Corinthians 4:7) is the NT expression of the same principle.
10: A direct revelation: 'Therefore thus says the Lord GOD: Because it towered high and set its top among the clouds and its heart was proud of its height, I will give it into the hand of a mighty one of the nations.' The heart was proud of its height (gavah libbo begobho) — pride rooted not in false claims but in genuine greatness turned inward. Christ's teaching makes the same principle explicit: “Everyone who exalts himself will be humbled” (Luke 14:11). The inevitable, universal law of divine governance: pride precedes the fall, without exception. The cedar fell; Babylon fell; Rome fell; every proud empire falls. Christ alone — the humble one — is exalted permanently.
11: A pattern: 'He dealt with it as its wickedness deserved; and a mighty one of the nations cut it down and left it.' The cedar “dealt with as its wickedness deserved” — the lex talionis of cosmic history. All wickedness receives its due; no proud empire escapes its accounting. This is the bedrock of the prophetic vision: history is not chaos but moral governance. Christ is the judge before whose throne all accounting is made final (Revelation 20:12-13).
12: A structural note: 'Foreigners, the most ruthless of nations, have cut it down and left it. On the mountains and in all the valleys its branches have fallen and its boughs are broken in all the ravines of the land, and all the peoples of the earth have gone away from its shadow and left it.' The cedar falls and all who sheltered in it flee — the empire's collapse leaves its dependents exposed. Only the one who shelters in YHWH is safe when empires fall (Psalms 46:1-3: “God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble. Therefore we will not fear though the earth gives way”).
13: A structural note: 'On its fallen trunk all the birds of the heavens settled, and on its branches all the beasts of the field took shelter.' The same birds and beasts that nested in the living cedar now dwell on the dead trunk. What was life-giving shelter has become a resting place for scavengers. The scavengers on the dead cedar echo the great feast of judgment (Revelation 19:17-18; Ezekiel 39:17-20). The contrast with Christ's resurrection is stark: his body did not see corruption (Acts 2:27, citing Psalms 16:10).
14: A direct revelation: 'This is in order that no trees by the waters may grow to towering height or set their tops among the clouds, and that no trees that drink water may reach up to them in height. For they are all given over to death, to the world below, among the children of man, with those who go down to the pit.' “No trees by the waters may grow to towering height” — the cedar's fall is a cosmic warning. All flesh is mortal (Isaiah 40:6). Christ alone was given what the cedar grasped: “All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me” (Matthew 28:18) — the authority the cedar seized, Christ received as gift from the Father.
15: A pattern: 'Thus says the Lord GOD: On the day it went down to Sheol I caused mourning; I covered the deep over it and restrained its rivers, and the many waters were stopped. I clothed Lebanon in gloom for it and all the trees of the field fainted because of it.' The whole natural order mourns the cedar's fall. Creation witnesses the event. This mourning of nature at the fall of the proud inverts the creation-mourning at the cross (darkness, earthquake, Matthew 27:45, 51): at the cross, creation mourned the fall of the righteous one; here it mourns the fall of the proud one. The same natural signals carry opposite theological weights.
16: A direct revelation: 'I made the nations quake at the sound of its fall when I cast it down to Sheol with those who go down to the pit. And all the trees of Eden, the choice and best of Lebanon, all that drink water, were comforted in the world below.' The trees of Eden in Sheol are “comforted” — the previously fallen powers recognize the greatest empire has joined them. This is the anti-comfort of Sheol: the company of the dead does not console. The contrast with Christ's resurrection comfort is total: “Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies and God of all comfort” (2 Corinthians 1:3). The comfort Christ gives is resurrection-comfort, not Sheol-comfort.
17: A structural note: 'They also went down to Sheol with it, to those who are slain by the sword; yes, those who were its arm, who lived under its shadow among the nations.' Those who sheltered in Egypt share its fate — they go down to Sheol with the cedar. The pattern is the anti-type of Christ's resurrection: those who are “in Christ” share his resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:22: “as in Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive”). Being in Egypt means sharing Egypt's Sheol-descent; being in Christ means sharing Christ's life.
18: A direct revelation: 'Whom are you thus like in glory and in greatness among the trees of Eden? You shall be brought down with the trees of Eden to the world below. You shall lie among the uncircumcised, with those who are slain by the sword. This is Pharaoh and all his multitude, declares the Lord GOD.' The oracle ends with Pharaoh identified as the great cedar. The contrast with Christ could not be more complete: Pharaoh goes down with the uncircumcised dead; Christ descends to the dead but rises, “having the keys of Death and Hades” (Revelation 1:18). The one who goes down to Sheol in judgment goes down permanently; the one who descends in victory rises and reigns.
